Notes: First petawatt laser; paper reports 1.5 PW.
Description: Nova Petawatt was an Nd:glass CPA laser created by converting one beamline of the Nova facility at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory. It started operation in 1996 and served as the first major demonstration that existing high-energy fusion-laser infrastructure could be adapted for ultra-short-pulse experiments.

Vulcan Petawatt at the Rutherford Appleton Laboratory was the first petawatt-class laser operated as a dedicated international user facility. It is an Nd:glass CPA system delivering roughly 500 J in 500 fs, synchronized with long-pulse beams for high-energy-density and laser-plasma experiments.

Notes: Paper reports 2.1 kJ at 10 ps and “almost 1 PW” at best compression.
Description: OMEGA-EP at the University of Rochester’s Laboratory for Laser Energetics added short-pulse petawatt capability to the OMEGA laser environment. Its short-pulse beams can operate from sub-picosecond to tens of picoseconds, with multi-kilojoule operation at longer pulse durations and near-petawatt performance at best compression.

Vulcan Petawatt is an Nd:glass CPA beamline at the Central Laser Facility at Rutherford Appleton Laboratory in the UK. It began petawatt user-facility operation in 2004 and supports high-energy-density and laser-plasma experiments synchronized with long-pulse Vulcan beams.

Notes: Eight short-pulse beams from one NIF quad.
Description: NIF ARC is the Advanced Radiographic Capability on NIF, using four NIF beams split into eight short-pulse beamlets. Each beamlet can deliver hundreds of joules to kilojoule-class energy over picosecond pulse durations, providing petawatt-class X-ray radiography for dense plasma experiments.

HAPLS / L3 was developed by LLNL for ELI-Beamlines as a high-average-power petawatt laser. It uses diode-pumped solid-state pump lasers driving Ti:sapphire CPA and was designed for 30 J, 30 fs pulses at 10 Hz, with early commissioning around 0.5 PW at 3.3 Hz.

Rep. rate: low rep.; 50 TW at 10 Hz mode
Description: J-KAREN at QST/KPSI in Japan was one of the landmark Ti:sapphire petawatt systems and the world’s first petawatt-class Ti:sapphire facility. It demonstrated 0.85 PW from 28.4 J, 33 fs pulses in 2003, with lower-power high-repetition-rate operating modes also available.

BELLA at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory was built for laser-plasma accelerator experiments. It became especially important as the first petawatt laser operating at 1 Hz, demonstrating that high-peak-power systems could support more accelerator-like experimental operation.

CSU Advanced Beam Laboratory operates a high-repetition-rate Ti:sapphire laser at Colorado State University. The paper lists it as a 0.85 PW system operating at 3.3 Hz, using a novel flashlamp-pumped Nd:glass pump-laser architecture.

Diocles at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln is a multi-mode Ti:sapphire laser facility used for high-intensity laser-plasma and radiation-source experiments. It came online first at 100 TW and later reached 0.7 PW at 0.1 Hz, while also supporting 100 TW and 6 TW higher-repetition-rate modes.

Laser technology: OPCPA front end + mixed Nd:glass CPA
Description: Texas Petawatt at the University of Texas at Austin combines an OPCPA front end with mixed Nd:glass amplification to shorten pulses compared with traditional glass petawatt systems. It delivered 186 J in 167 fs, about 1.1 PW, and was optimized for high-intensity focused interactions.

Scarlet at Ohio State University is a Ti:sapphire dual-CPA laser built for relativistic plasma and high-energy-density physics. It delivers about 15 J in 40 fs, reported as 400 TW, with one shot per minute and high temporal contrast from XPW-based pulse cleaning.

Notes: Paper reports 200 TW original, 500 TW upgraded.
Description: ALLS at INRS/University of Quebec is a commercial Ti:sapphire PULSAR system used for ultrafast high-intensity science. The paper describes it as originally operating around 200 TW and later upgraded to 500 TW with 10 J, 20 fs pulses.

Gemini at the Rutherford Appleton Laboratory is a two-beam Ti:sapphire user facility. Each beamline delivers about 15 J in 30 fs at 800 nm, giving 500 TW per beam and enabling synchronized dual-beam experiments at high focused intensity.

Notes: Also has 400 TW green high-contrast mode.
Description: Orion at AWE Aldermaston combines long-pulse Nd:glass beams with two synchronized infrared petawatt beams. Each petawatt beam can deliver about 500 J in 500 fs, and one beamline can also operate in a frequency-doubled high-contrast green mode.

Notes: Europe’s highest average-power commercial petawatt-scale laser at paper time.
Description: SCAPA at the University of Strathclyde is a commercial Thales Ti:sapphire laser designed for plasma-based accelerator applications. It delivers 8.75 J in 25 fs, about 350 TW, at 5 Hz, making it a high-average-power petawatt-scale facility in Europe.

PETAL is the short-pulse beamline coupled to France’s Laser Megajoule facility. It is a multi-kilojoule Nd:glass CPA system; the paper reports initial commissioning performance of 850 J in 700 fs, or 1.15 PW.

Apollon in Saclay is a next-generation French multi-petawatt facility based on hybrid OPCPA and Ti:sapphire amplification. It is designed for 150 J in 15 fs, or 10 PW, and had demonstrated 1 PW operation by the time discussed in the paper.

LOA 200 TW at Laboratoire d’Optique Appliquee is a commercial Ti:sapphire laser used for laser-plasma acceleration and multi-particle source studies. It delivers about 6 J in 30 fs at 1 Hz, corresponding to roughly 200 TW.

PHELIX is uniquely integrated with GSI's heavy-ion accelerator complex, enabling synchronized laser–ion pump–probe experiments for investigations of warm dense matter, plasma physics, and matter under extreme conditions. The facility supports laser-driven secondary source development—including protons, electrons, neutrons, and X-rays—and has played a significant role in advancing high-energy-density physics through combined laser and accelerator capabilities.

Peak power: 200 TW on target; 0.55 PW est. from energy/duration
Description: POLARIS is distinguished as one of the world's first fully diode-pumped, high-power ultrafast laser systems, demonstrating the scalability and efficiency of diode-pumped petawatt-class technology. The facility primarily supports research in laser-driven particle acceleration, relativistic laser–plasma interactions, and the development of next-generation high-repetition-rate high-intensity laser architectures.

DRACO (Dresden Laser Acceleration Source) is a user facility dedicated to relativistic laser–plasma interaction research, with particular emphasis on laser-wakefield electron acceleration, ion acceleration, and secondary radiation source generation. Its close integration with HZDR's ELBE accelerator infrastructure enables advanced beam diagnostics and studies that bridge conventional accelerator physics with plasma-based acceleration techniques.

JETI200 is optimized for ultrashort-pulse experiments requiring exceptional temporal contrast, achieved through a double chirped-pulse amplification architecture with cross-polarized wave generation. The system serves as a versatile platform for studies of relativistic laser–matter interactions, laser-driven electron and ion acceleration, and high-harmonic generation, while also supporting the development of advanced target and diagnostic technologies.

Notes: Three-line VEGA system; VEGA-3 is PW line.
Description: VEGA-3 is the flagship beamline of Spain's VEGA laser system and forms part of a synchronized multi-beam architecture that enables flexible pump–probe and multi-pulse experimental configurations. As an international user facility, it supports research in laser-driven particle acceleration, secondary radiation sources, warm dense matter, and high-intensity laser–plasma physics, while emphasizing high repetition rate operation and broad experimental accessibility.

CETAL (Center for Advanced Laser Technologies) was established as Romania's national petawatt-class laser facility to support research in high-field laser physics, laser–plasma interactions, advanced photonics, and materials science. In addition to serving as a platform for fundamental research, the facility provides a technological foundation for developing high-power laser instrumentation and complements the larger European-scale capabilities available at ELI-NP.

ELI-NP is the world's first operational dual-arm 10 PW laser facility, designed specifically to enable experiments at the intersection of ultra-intense laser science and nuclear physics. By combining two synchronized 10 PW beamlines with a high-brilliance gamma-beam source, the facility supports frontier research in laser-driven nuclear reactions, nonlinear quantum electrodynamics, particle acceleration, and laboratory astrophysics, while operating as an international user facility.

PEARL (PEtawatt pARametric Laser) pioneered the use of optical parametric chirped-pulse amplification (OPCPA) for generating high-energy ultrashort pulses, demonstrating an alternative to conventional Ti:sapphire petawatt architectures. The facility has served as a testbed for high-contrast pulse generation and investigations of relativistic laser–matter interactions, while informing the development of subsequent multi-petawatt OPCPA systems worldwide.

Notes: Pump constraints limited output to 1 PW.
Description: FEMTA is a high-intensity femtosecond laser facility developed to support research in relativistic laser–matter interactions, laser-driven particle acceleration, nonlinear optics, and ultrafast plasma physics. The system also serves as a platform for developing advanced laser technologies and diagnostics, providing a flexible experimental environment for investigating high-field phenomena and validating concepts relevant to next-generation high-power laser facilities.

The SG-II 5 PW OPCPA system was developed to demonstrate the scalability of optical parametric chirped-pulse amplification to multi-petawatt peak powers while maintaining excellent pulse contrast and broad amplification bandwidth. The facility supports research in relativistic laser–plasma interactions, laboratory astrophysics, high-energy-density physics, and strong-field quantum electrodynamics, and has served as a key technology demonstrator for China's next generation of ultra-intense laser facilities.

Qiangguang was developed to investigate relativistic laser–plasma interactions and the generation of laser-driven particle and radiation sources at ultra-high intensities. The facility has also served as a testbed for advancing high-field laser technologies, including improvements in pulse contrast, beam quality, and target interaction physics that support next-generation ultra-intense laser systems.

This hybrid laser architecture combines conventional chirped-pulse amplification (CPA) with optical parametric chirped-pulse amplification (OPCPA) to leverage the high energy scalability of CPA and the broad bandwidth and high temporal contrast of OPCPA. The system was developed as a technology demonstrator for future multi-petawatt lasers, validating hybrid amplification strategies that have since been incorporated into higher-power facilities such as SULF.

The SULF prototype served as the precursor to the full Shanghai Superintense Ultrafast Laser Facility, demonstrating key technologies required for reliable 10 PW-class operation, including high-energy Ti:sapphire amplification, pulse compression, and ultrahigh temporal contrast. Its successful commissioning established the technological foundation for the operational SULF user facility, which supports research in strong-field physics, high-energy-density science, particle acceleration, and ultrafast materials science.

CAEP-PW is a multi-petawatt laser facility developed to investigate relativistic laser–matter interactions, laboratory astrophysics, and high-energy-density physics under extreme electromagnetic fields. The system emphasizes high pulse quality and temporal contrast, providing a platform for experiments in laser-driven particle acceleration, secondary radiation source generation, and strong-field plasma physics while contributing to the development of China's next generation of ultra-intense laser technology.

XL-III is a petawatt-class Ti:sapphire laser facility designed for studies of ultrafast, relativistic laser–plasma interactions and the production of high-brightness secondary particle and photon sources. The facility has been extensively used to investigate laser-driven electron and ion acceleration, high-order harmonic generation, and nonlinear strong-field phenomena, while also serving as a platform for developing advanced diagnostics and high-intensity laser techniques.

SILEX-I was developed as an early optical parametric chirped-pulse amplification (OPCPA) platform to investigate the generation of high-energy, ultrashort pulses with excellent temporal contrast and broad amplification bandwidth. The facility played an important role in maturing all-OPCPA technologies and informed the subsequent development of higher-power systems within the XingGuang (XG) laser program, including the SILEX-II multi-petawatt platform.

Notes: Paper gives 0.7 PW and duration; energy estimated.
Description: The XG-III femtosecond beam is an ultrashort-pulse beamline within the XingGuang-III laser platform, designed to support pump–probe experiments and relativistic laser–plasma interaction studies relevant to inertial confinement fusion and high-energy-density physics. Its integration with long-pulse capabilities enables time-resolved investigations of matter under extreme conditions, providing a versatile platform for laser-driven particle acceleration and secondary radiation source generation.

Notes: First petawatt class laser in Asia.
Description: The petawatt beam added to the GEKKO XII laser facility was developed to investigate the fast-ignition approach to inertial confinement fusion by combining ultrashort, ultra-intense heating pulses with long-pulse target compression. Beyond fusion research, the beamline has enabled studies in relativistic laser–plasma interactions, high-energy-density physics, and laser-driven particle acceleration, establishing GEKKO XII as one of the pioneering hybrid long- and short-pulse laser facilities.

Notes: Paper reports current 2 PW in 1 ps.
Description: LFEX was specifically developed to deliver multi-kilojoule, picosecond laser pulses for fast-ignition inertial confinement fusion experiments in conjunction with the GEKKO XII compression laser. In addition to advancing fast-ignition research, the facility supports investigations of relativistic plasma physics, high-energy-density science, and intense laser–matter interactions at extreme energy densities.

J-KAREN-P is a high-contrast petawatt Ti:sapphire laser optimized for experiments requiring exceptional pulse fidelity, making it particularly well suited for relativistic laser–plasma interactions, laser-driven ion and electron acceleration, and high-field quantum electrodynamics. The facility has been widely used to develop advanced target technologies and precision diagnostics, while serving as one of Japan's flagship user facilities for ultra-intense laser science.

Notes: Two 0.5 PW beams; user mode initially reduced.
Description: The HERMES laser is an ultrafast optical laser system synchronized with the SACLA X-ray free-electron laser (XFEL), enabling femtosecond pump–probe experiments that combine ultra-intense optical excitation with coherent hard X-ray diagnostics. This integrated platform supports investigations of ultrafast structural dynamics, high-field light–matter interactions, nonlinear X-ray physics, and strong-field quantum electrodynamics, leveraging the exceptional temporal synchronization between the optical and XFEL beamlines.

Laser technology: Ti:sapphire CPA with XPW/OPA front end
Notes: Upgraded from earlier 1.1 PW and 1.5 PW beamlines.
Description: The CoReLS 4 PW laser was developed to achieve extreme focused intensities through advanced wavefront correction and adaptive optics, becoming the first laser system to experimentally exceed an intensity of 1 × 10^23 W/cm^2. The facility is a leading platform for strong-field quantum electrodynamics, relativistic laser-plasma interactions, laser-driven particle acceleration, and laboratory astrophysics, with ongoing research into nonlinear Compton scattering, Breit-Wheeler pair production, and radiation-pressure ion acceleration.

Notes: Paper calls it upgraded to 1 PW.
Description: EXLS is a conceptual exawatt-class laser architecture proposed to investigate physical regimes beyond those accessible with current multi-petawatt facilities. The design explores coherent beam combination and advanced pulse compression technologies to enable experiments in nonlinear quantum electrodynamics, vacuum pair production, laboratory astrophysics, and other extreme-field phenomena anticipated at exawatt intensities.

Notes: Paper also notes existing 150 TW, 5 Hz system.
Description: The RRCAT petawatt laser was developed as India's flagship ultrafast high-intensity laser facility to support research in relativistic laser–matter interactions, laser-driven particle acceleration, high-energy-density physics, and secondary radiation source generation. In addition to enabling fundamental studies in strong-field science, the facility provides a national platform for advancing indigenous high-power laser technologies and ultrafast diagnostics.

Institution: Shanghai Institute of Optics and Fine Mechanics (SIOM) / ShanghaiTech University
Raja Ramanna Centre for Advanced Technology (RRCAT), Indore
